paragraphs: RentWise
A rental platform designed to help Nigerians find trustworthy homes faster and help landlords list properties with less friction.

Role: Lead UX Designer
Timeline: Aug 2024 - Dec 2024
Scope: Research, UX strategy, wireframing, prototyping, usability testing
Overview

Finding a house in Nigeria should not feel like a gamble. But for many renters, it does. Fake listings, agent fees, scattered information, and unclear next steps make the process stressful and expensive. On the other side, many landlords still avoid digital tools because listing platforms often feel too technical or tedious.
RentWise was designed to solve both problems: make the rental journey clearer and more trustworthy for tenants, while making digital listing easier for landlords.
Problem Statement
Business problem

The rental market is still fragmented and heavily dependent on manual agents, which slows down digital adoption and reduces trust in property platforms. If landlords do not feel comfortable listing online, supply remains limited and marketplace growth suffers.
User problem

Renters struggle to know which listings are real, which prices are accurate, and which actions to take next. Many lose time comparing scattered information or paying for agent-led processes that add cost without enough value.
Discovery Research

I began with interviews and surveys across Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt to understand what really breaks the rental experience. The research showed that the problem was not only search. It was trust. Users were not just asking, “Can I find a house?” They were asking, “Can I trust what I am seeing?” The study plan also defined the core research goals around search, filtering, application flow, confidence, and trust.
What I found
Four themes appeared consistently in the early research:

Fake listings
Renters found it hard to trust property photos and listing accuracy.

Agent dependency
Many users were frustrated by agent fees, especially when the value was unclear.

Information overload
Property information was often scattered, inconsistent, or outdated, making comparison difficult.
This changed the direction of the product. RentWise was no longer just a listing app. It became a product focused on clarity, trust, and direct connection.
Personas
Chinedu, First-Time Renter

Chinedu wants a simple way to search for homes, compare options, and contact landlords directly without paying unnecessary fees. He needs verified information, clear pricing, and a low-stress process.
Renter persona behind this journey

This journey is built around a renter like Chinedu, a young working professional searching for a trustworthy apartment in a busy city like Lagos. He wants to avoid fake listings, reduce agent-related stress, compare options easily, and make a confident decision without wasting time or money.
Key emotional drop points
The biggest emotional dips happen at:
Search, when too many listings feel unclear or irrelevant
Inspection, when trust is tested and the renter fears listing mismatch
Decision/Application, where hidden costs, unclear terms, and payment risk create the highest stress
Most important product opportunities
The map shows three major product priorities:
Trust-building through verification, transparent fees, and better listing quality
Decision support through comparison tools, complete property details, and clearer cost visibility
Process clarity through guided applications, secure payments, and a structured move-in experience
User Flows
I mapped the renter journeys to understand where friction happened before moving into interface decisions.
Wireframes to High-Fidelity
I started with paper wireframes to explore layout direction and simplify the overall flow. Early concepts focused on reducing clutter, improving navigation, and making key actions easier to spot.

From there, I moved into low-fidelity screens to test structure and interaction logic. The final high-fidelity prototype introduced stronger trust cues, cleaner navigation, and more supportive landlord onboarding.
The visual direction stayed clean and modern, but every major UI choice came from a real usability problem, not just aesthetics.
Design Decisions
1) Filters were too easy to miss

Issue: Renters ignored or struggled to find the filter controls early in the flow.
Decision: I introduced a sticky filter bar with clearer labels and stronger hierarchy.
Why: Users needed faster control over budget and property type without scanning the whole page.
2) The interface felt too icon-heavy

Issue: Too many icons made the UI feel noisy and harder to interpret.
Decision: I reduced icon density and relied more on text labels and clear section headings.
Why: This lowered cognitive load and made navigation easier to understand at a glance.
3) Renters wanted to save listings

Issue: Users wanted a way to shortlist homes before contacting landlords.
Decision: I added a Favorites feature for bookmarking and comparing listings.
Why: Comparing properties is a natural part of renting, and forcing immediate action increased hesitation.
Usability Testing
I conducted remote usability testing with 10 renters Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt. Participants completed key tasks like browsing listings and creating a listing.

The main issues were clear: users missed the filter bar, too many icons made the interface feel noisy, and renters wanted a way to save listings. These findings directly informed the next round of design improvements.
Results
Observed results from testing
The revised prototype moved RentWise closer to a more trustworthy and easier rental experience.
The most important thing the data showed was this: the core opportunity was not simply helping users search for properties. It was reducing hesitation at every decision point.
By the end of testing, the clearest strengths of the concept were:
users understood the value of verified listings
renters wanted direct landlord access
the product direction strongly matched a real market problem
Key numbers
Browse/filter completion: 80%
Save/apply completion: 70%
Average SUS: 63.1
Average confidence: 3.1 / 5
Projected results after the design changes
Based on the changes made after testing, the next iteration is expected to improve to:
Browse/filter completion: from 80% → 90%
Save/apply completion: from 70% → 85%
Average confidence: from 3.1 → 4.2
Average SUS: from 63.1 → 98
Reflection
This project taught me that trust is not a feature you add at the end. It has to be built into the entire flow, from search to contact to listing creation.
I also learned that a two-sided marketplace cannot succeed by solving for renters alone. Making landlord onboarding easier is just as important as making property discovery better.

If I took RentWise further, I would focus on a mobile-first MVP, test stronger listing verification cues, add secure payments and rent tracking, and support landlords with bulk upload tools and digital lease workflows.
